coredata.dc:title: Amino acid derivative-based Ln-metallohydrogels with multi-stimuli responsiveness and applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yang
Wei, Chuan-Wan
Wang, Xiao-Juan
Gao, Shu-Qin
Lin, Ying-Wu
coredata.dc:description: Metallohydrogels and lanthanide (Ln) fluorescent materials have gained much attention recently. In this study, we designed and synthesized a facile gelator of a phenylalanine-based derivative containing an indazole group (namely IZF). It was found that IZF can self-assemble to form hydrogel at pH ≤ 7. Meanwhile, IZF and Tb3+/Eu3+ can co-assemble to generate IZF-Tb and IZF-Eu metallohydrogels with green and red fluorescence, respectively, at pH 8–11, with excellent multi-stimuli responsiveness. The bimetallic hydrogels of IZF-Tb/Eu exhibit different colors under UV light by adjusting the ratio of Tb3+ and Eu3+. Moreover, white light emission was achieved with IZF-Tb/Eu bimetallic gels through doping carbon dots (CDs) by tailoring the stoichiometric ratio of Ln-complex and CDs. Remarkably, IZF-Tb and IZF-Eu could be used as fluorescent inks with excellent stability. This study indicates that the amino acid derivative-based Ln-metallohydrogels are excellent candidates for constructing information storage and multiple anti-counterfeiting materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amino acid derivative
Ln-metallohydrogels
Multi-stimuli responsiveness
Fluorescent inks
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The structure of IZF, the digital photos of (b) IZF gel (c) IZF-Eu gel, (d) IZF-Tb gel, (e) IZF-Eu/Tb-CDs gel under sunlight and UV light (254 nm), and (f) IZF-Eu/Tb bimetal hydrogel with different ratio of Eu/Tb under UV light (254 nm).
The multi-stimuli responsive properties of IZF-Tb metallohydrogel.

Digital photos of pH response: (a) IZF-Tb metallohydrogel under daylight and under UV light, (b) IZF-Tb xerogel exposure to HCl/NH3 vapor under UV light (at 254 nm), (c) Fluorescent spectra at different pH (from 8 to 2), and (d) changes in fluorescent intensity (at 550 nm) during four consecutive cycles of adding acid and base.
(a) Frequency sweep rheological analysis of IZF-Tb metallohydrogel at a constant strain of 0.1%. (b) Step-strain time dependent rheological analysis of IZF-Tb metallohydrogel at frequency of 1 Hz.
The SEM and TEM images of IZF (a and b) and IZF-Tb (c and d) xerogels, respectively.
Fluorescence spectra of IZF (1 × 10−4 mol∙L−1) changed with the concentration of Tb3+ ions.
(a) FT-IR spectra, (b) XRD patterns of IZF and IZF-Tb xerogels.

(a) A flower with “IZF-Tb” ink coding leaf cadres and “IZF-Eu” ink encoding flower part; (b) The use of “IZF-Tb” as a QR code image engraved on a paper with green fluorescent ink. A smartphone successfully read the QD code process under 254 nm ultraviolet light; (c) The hidden information of “IOUSC” was triply encrypted with the help of the ASCII code.

head.ce:title: Amino acid derivative-based Ln-metallohydrogels with multi-stimuli responsiveness and applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of South China, Hengyang 421001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of South China, Hengyang 421001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab of Protein Structure and Function of Universities in Human Province, University of South China, Hengyang 421001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab of Protein Structure and Function of Universities in Human Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Lab of Protein Structure and Function of Universities in Human Province, University of South China, Hengyang 421001, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of South China, Hengyang 421001, China (Y.-W. Lin).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metallohydrogels and lanthanide (Ln) fluorescent materials have gained much attention recently. In this study, we designed and synthesized a facile gelator of a phenylalanine-based derivative containing an indazole group (namely IZF). It was found that IZF can self-assemble to form hydrogel at pH ≤ 7. Meanwhile, IZF and Tb3+/Eu3+ can co-assemble to generate IZF-Tb and IZF-Eu metallohydrogels with green and red fluorescence, respectively, at pH 8–11, with excellent multi-stimuli responsiveness. The bimetallic hydrogels of IZF-Tb/Eu exhibit different colors under UV light by adjusting the ratio of Tb3+ and Eu3+. Moreover, white light emission was achieved with IZF-Tb/Eu bimetallic gels through doping carbon dots (CDs) by tailoring the stoichiometric ratio of Ln-complex and CDs. Remarkably, IZF-Tb and IZF-Eu could be used as fluorescent inks with excellent stability. This study indicates that the amino acid derivative-based Ln-metallohydrogels are excellent candidates for constructing information storage and multiple anti-counterfeiting materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Supramolecular hydrogels constructed by low molecular weight gelators have gained enormous attention [1–6], which are important soft materials containing a large number of water molecules among the 3D fibrillar networks and can be applied in many fields, especially in biology [7–11]. These hydrogels are assembled through various non-covalent interactions, such as H-bonding, π–π stacking, and hydrophobic interactions, as well as van der Waals [12]. In addition to these interactions, metallohydrogels are formed through metal–ligand interactions [13], which are a branch of supramolecular hydrogels and contain metallic elements in the fiber as a structural component. The introduction of metal ions confers the hydrogels additional properties and functions such as optical, electrical, magnetic, redox properties, and catalytic functions [14].

Lanthanide complexes formed through metal–ligand interactions have aroused considerable attention because they exhibit excellent optical properties such as large Stokes shifts, narrow emission bands, and long luminescence lifetime [15–17]. Lanthanide coordination polymers have been used in many fields including lighting, imaging, sensing, and anti-counterfeiting, etc, due to their excellent photoluminescence and stimuli-responsive properties [18–21]. However, the luminescence of the lanthanide complexes was affected by various factors such as mechanical stimulus, temperature, the preparation procedure and requirement of organic solvents, which resulted in limited applications [22–24]. To overcome these shortcomings, lanthanide complexes were embedded in various matrices [25,26]. Meanwhile, the luminescence disappeared when the matrices were destroyed [27]. Therefore, it is vital to design versatile ligands to construct stable lanthanide-containing fluorescent materials.

Moreover, to solve the optical instability of lanthanide fluorescent materials, a strategy can be proposed through encapsulating lanthanide ions as a structural component in gel matrices. Meanwhile, most of the reported lanthanide metallogels are formed in organic solvents [28–30], which limit their applications. Therefore, it still remains a great challenge for the design of low molecular weight gelator-based lanthanide metallohydrogels with multi-stimuli responsiveness, avoiding the use of any organic solvents.

Amino acid derivatives have good biocompatibility, low toxicity and simple preparation procedure compared to other organic ligands [31–37]. In addition, the constructed ligand needs both good coordination ability and the ability to provide a variety of non-covalent interactions [38]. For these considerations, we designed and constructed a facile gelator of a phenylalanine-based derivative containing an indazole group (named IZF). It can not only trigger the coordination but also provide H-bonding and π–π stacking interactions, which thus may meet the requirements for the formation of the complexes and hydrogels. As shown herein, we obtained IZF-Ln metallogels in an aqueous solution with stable and unique optical properties, which could be used as information storage and security materials. Moreover, bimetallic hydrogels (IZF-Eu/Tb) with different emission and hybrid metallohydrogel with white light emission (IZF-Eu/Tb-CDs) was fabricated. To the best of our knowledge, the facile gelator and functional IZF-Ln metallohydrogels have not been reported yet.

As synthesized, the obtained IZF (Fig. 1 a, Scheme S1, Figs. S1 and S2) was yellow powder, which was soluble in alkaline aqueous solution and did not form hydrogel even if it was placed for a long time or treated with sonication. However, IZF can self-assemble to form yellow hydrogels by shaking at room temperature (RT) under the acid condition (pH ≤ 7) (Fig. 1b), likely due to the formation of strong H-bonding and π-π stacking interactions under this condition. Moreover, IZF and Eu3+/Tb3+ can co-assemble to generate metallohydrogels (namely, IZF-Eu, and IZF-Tb, respectively) with ultrasonic treatment within several minutes by regulating the pH of IZF to 8–11 (Fig. 1c and d). Under the alkaline condition, the coordination ability of IZF may be enhanced as a result of the deprotonation, which is helpful for metal–ligand coordination, and the resulting coordination complex may be further cross-linked to form metallohydrogels. When pH exceeded 12, the metal ions formed hydroxide deposits and were thus not conductive to gel formation.

Intriguingly, the hybrid metallohydrogel (IZF-Eu/Tb-CDs) was also constructed by doping CDs to the complex (Fig. 1e). We studied the influence of anions on the gelation behavior and found that the mixture of IZF with different lanthanide salts (i.e. Ln(NO3)3, Ln2(SO4)3, LnCl3) can generate hydrogels under the same conditions, which suggests that the gelling process was almost unaffected by anions. Moreover, we studied the gelling performance of L-IZF, D-IZF and the racemic mixture (L + D)-IZF. The results showed that the chirality of the gelator has no effect on the gelling behavior. When these metallogels were placed under UV light (254 nm), they exhibited different luminescence, while IZF gels have almost no fluorescence. Interestingly, IZF-Eu and IZF-Tb present respective characteristic luminescence of red and green, respectively.

Note that bimetallic hydrogels IZF-Eu/Tb can also be obtained through controlling the stoichiometry of Eu3+/Tb3+, with color changes continuously from red (10:0 ratio) to green (0:10 ratio) by the increase of the Tb3+ ratio under UV light (254 nm) (Fig. 1f). Especially, the IZF-Eu/Tb metallohydrogel showed yellow when the ratio of Eu3+ and Tb3+ was 8:2. Therefore, based on the principle of three primary colors, IZF-Eu/Tb-CDs hybrid metallohydrogel with white light emission was obtained by mixing CDs in the yellow IZF-Eu/Tb metallohydrogel (Fig. 1e). We will further discuss the characteristics of IZF-Tb gel in the following section.

Multi-stimuli responsiveness is a key feature of smart soft materials. As shown in Fig. 2 , the IZF-Tb metallohydrogel exhibited multi-stimuli responsive properties to various chemical and physical stimuli. For example, the gel was transformed into sol through vigorous shaking by hand, and then the sol was restored to a gel rapidly, indicating a rapid thixotropic response. Addition of solid crystalline EDTA (Ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid sodium) to the IZF-Tb gel resulted in the collapse of gel and the quenching of the fluorescence, owing to the strong coordination of EDTA with Tb3+ ions. When the fresh IZF-Tb gel was heated to 50 °C, the mild collapse was observed with a decrease in fluorescence, which recovered to self-supported gel and original fluorescence by placing at RT (room temperature). The results suggest that the IZF-Tb gel possesses reversible thermal responsiveness and the temperature has a significant influence on the fluorescence of the IZF-Tb gel. We further studied the fluorescent spectra of IZF-Tb under different temperatures. As shown in Fig. S3, the fluorescent intensity decreased by increasing the temperature, with a linear correlation between the fluorescent intensity at 550 nm and the temperature in a range from 15 to 75 °C.

Remarkably, the IZF-Tb metallohydrogel showed excellent acid-base responsiveness (Fig. 3 ). The addition of HCl into the gel triggered the transformation from gel to sol, which was quickly restored to gel by adding NaOH. The IZF-Tb metallohydrogel showed green fluorescence under UV light (254 nm). The fluorescent intensity of the gel weakened distinctly after adding HCl, and recovered after the addition of NaOH (Fig. 3a). Interestingly, IZF-Tb xerogel powder had similar behavior for the “on–off” luminescence switching behavior when exposed to HCl vapor or NH3 vapor (Fig. 3b). The fluorescence of IZF-Tb powder could be switched off by exposure to HCl gas for several minutes, and the fluorescence appeared by treatment with NH3 vapor. The pH reversible responsiveness of IZF-Tb was further studied by successive addition of HCl and NaOH into the diluted IZF-Tb gel. The emission fluorescent spectra Fig. 3c showed that the addition of HCl into IZF-Tb induced an obvious fluorescence decrease, whereas the fluorescence almost recovered to the original state after the addition of the same amount of NaOH. Moreover, the process could be repeated at least four cycles (Fig. 3d), suggesting the excellent pH responsiveness of IZF-Tb. The on–off switching behavior of fluorescence is likely due to the competition between the coordination interaction of IZF and Tb3+ ions and the protonation of IZF. The IZF-Tb complex may become unstable as a result of the protonation of IZF when exposed to HCl vapor (or adding HCl to IZF-Tb gel), leading to the disappearance of the fluorescence. Meanwhile, NH3 vapor (or NaOH) could induce the complete coordination interaction between IZF and Tb3+ ions by deprotonation, and the fluorescence of IZF-Tb could thus be restored.

To gain insights into the mechanical strength and the thixotropic nature of the prepared IZF-Tb metallohydrogel, we carried out various rheological studies. A dynamic frequency sweep (DFS) experiment was performed. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the storage modulus (G’) and loss modulus (G’’) did not vary significantly and the average value of G’ was higher than G’’ throughout the experimental region, which indicates the presence of a stable and rigid gel phase material. Dynamic time sweep (DTS) measurements further confirmed the stability of the IZF-Tb hydrogel (Fig. S4). The above responsiveness study has shown that IZF-Tb gel possesses good thixotropy by shaking-resting test (Fig. 2). Here, the thixotropy was further confirmed by a time-dependent step-strain loop test involving consecutive cycles of low–high strains. The results showed that the mechanical properties of the fresh gels were almost recovered within 3 min after termination of the large stress, which is consistent with the result of the shaking-resting test. Note that the process could be repeated at least five cycles (Fig. 4b and Fig. S5).

To explore the possible assembly mechanism of metallohydrogels, we performed a series of studies using different techniques including IR spectroscopy, SEM, TEM, XRD, EI-MS, UV–vis, CD, isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC), and fluorescence spectra. Herein, we take IZF-Tb as an example to elucidate the gelling mechanism. The electron microscopy was performed to obtain the microstructures of the prepared gels. From Fig. 5 a-b, it was observed that the self-assembled IZF gel was constructed with entangled fibers with several microns in length due to the existence of strong H-bonding interactions in acid conditions. Meanwhile, the microstructure of IZF-Tb gel presented fleet-like fibers stacking each other, which is different from that of IZF gel due to the coordination between IZF and Tb3 + ions (Fig. 5c-d). The coordination interactions occurred under basic conditions, which in turn decreased the protonation and H-bonding interactions. Therefore, the formation of metal–ligand interactions within the coordination complex changed the assembly state of the system and made the arrangement of the molecules more orderly via the intensified π-π stacking interactions, which is in accordance with the results of XRD. Note that the micromorphologies of IZF-Eu, IZF-Eu/Tb, and IZF-Eu/Tb-CDs metallohydrogels are similar to that of IZF-Tb gel (Fig. S6-8).

We also investigated the coordination interactions between IZF and Ln3+ ions in solution at low concentrations. Fig. 6 shows the luminescence spectra of IZF with an increasing concentration of Tb3+ upon excitation at 250 nm. The emission bands at 495, 550, 589 and 624 nm appeared with the addition of Tb3+, which is due to the deactivation of 5D4 excited state of Tb (III) to the 7FJ (J = 6–3) ground states (5D4 →7F6, 5D4 →7F5, 5D4 →7F4 and 5D4 →7F3) (Fig. S9). Similar observations were found in the IZF-Eu system (Fig. S10). The absorption band of IZF in an aqueous solution appeared at 260 nm and 290 nm due to the π-π* transition (Fig. S11). No significant changes were observed in the shapes of absorption spectra of IZF with the addition of Ln3+ ions and the absorbance band at 290 shifted slightly due to metal–ligand interaction between Ln3+ ions and IZF. It was observed that the CD spectrum of IZF-Ln was different from that of IZF (Fig. S12). These observations indicate that IZF and Ln3+ ions form a stable complex in the solution.

The coordination between IZF and Tb3+ was further probed by FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 7
a, the stretching vibration at 3360.2 cm−1 (ν-NH) was shifted to 3204.8 cm−1 in the complex, indicating the participation of the amino nitrogen atom in the coordination. Moreover, the characteristic peak of carbonyl at 1598.6 cm−1 was shifted to 1619.9 cm−1, and symmetric band (1401.1 cm−1) of CO in COO− was split into two peaks (1418 cm−1 and 1380.9 cm−1) [39], which suggests that the carboxyl oxygen atom was involved in the formation of IZF-Tb complex by the coordination. These results suggest that IZF ligand presumably provides both the carboxyl O atom and amino N atom for the coordination.

To further investigate the coordination mode between IZF and Ln ions, we performed various tests including Job’s plot, ESI-MS, and Isothermal Titration Calorimetry (ITC) studies. Job’s plot results showed that the binding stoichiometry between IZF and Tb3+ is 2 (Fig. S13), which is in accordance with the results of ESI-MS study (Fig. S14). The ITC results showed that the stoichiometry is ∼2.3, with a binding constant (K a) of 3.32 × 104 L/mol (Fig. S15). These results indicate that one Tb3+ ion is coordinated by two IZF molecules. Note that the coordination mode of IZF and Eu3+ is similar to that of IZF-Tb (Figs. S16-18).

The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) provides additional information for the gelling mechanism. As shown in Fig. 7(b), the IZF gel exhibited a broad diffraction peak at 20°, corresponding to the amorphous nature of gels [40], while the peak within IZF-Tb gel was weak. The SEM and TEM images showed that loose fiber networks were presented in IZF whereas dense fleet-like fibers were in IZF-Tb, in consistent with the results of XRD. Moreover, a typical peak of H-bonds (2.3 Å) was presented in the IZF gel. Nevertheless, the H-bonding interaction was decreased in IZF-Tb metallohydrogel owing to the deprotonation at high pH values. In addition, π-π stacking interactions (3.6 Å) were enhanced in the IZF-Tb gel [41]. Remarkably, a new peak appeared at ∼28° within the IZF-Tb gel, which was assigned to the metal–metal interactions. Therefore, these experimental results showed that the addition of Eu3+/Tb3+ to IZF could trigger the formation of the IZF-Ln complex, which could be further assembled to form metallohydrogels with a fibrous network structure by various non-covalent interactions.

The information storage code based on fluorescent materials has received widespread attention in the field of information storage and security. Recently, researchers have prepared a series of materials capable of information storage based on various gels with fluorescent properties. Usually, these fluorescent information storage codes are recognized only under UV light at specific wavelength [42], thus protecting information security. Inspired by these achievements, we herein used the IZF-Tb and IZF-Eu metallohydrogels as possible facile light-emitting ink. The IZF-Tb and IZF-Eu metallohydrogels encapsulate water molecules in fibrillar networks, which avoids dispersing the luminescent materials in organic solvents, and is thus environmental-friendly and easy to store. For example, flowers, dots, and English letters could be easily drawn using IZF-Tb and IZF-Eu with a writing brush, which suggests that IZF-Tb and IZF-Eu could be used as anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink. Especially, the strategy of double encryption and triple encryption was proposed to improve the anti-counterfeiting level of light-emitting ink.

As shown in Fig. 8a, a flower was written with IZF-Eu as the petal part of a flower, and IZF-Tb as the leaf and stem parts. The as-written flower was faint yellow under daylight, and the color difference of petal and leaf of the flower could not be distinguished directly. However, the flower image with red petals and green leaf could be seen clearly under UV light at 254 nm. These observations indicate that the IZF-Tb and IZF-Eu hydrogels can be used as luminescent ink directly for anti-counterfeiting.

The QR code is a daily means of information storage, which can further be improved for the function of anti-counterfeiting by using fluorescent ink materials (Fig. 8b). Here, the dual encryption strategy was performed by drawing a QR code pattern using IZF-Tb ink to improve the anti-counterfeiting level. The QR code can hardly be read in daylight whereas can be clearly seen under UV light. Moreover, the encrypted information can only be scanned and identified by the smartphone through the scan function, which can eventually be translated into the corresponding information “Eu and Tb” (Fig. 8b). These results showed that the decryption of QR codes requires UV light and the help of smartphones, which elucidates that the anti-counterfeiting function has been greatly improved through QR code strategy.

Then, the triple encryption was constructed through a two-color microarray using both IZF-Eu and IZF-Tb inks. As shown in Fig. 8c, almost nothing was seen on the paper under daylight, meanwhile, the green luminescent dots (standing for “1″) and the red luminescent dots (standing for “0”) presented clearly under UV light. Herein, the decryption process contains three steps. Firstly, UV light was needed to recognize the luminescent dots. Then, the red luminescent dots and green luminescent dots were changed into numbers “0” or “1”, respectively. And finally, the binary codes were translated into the letters “IOUSC” according to the ASCII codes, which is the abbreviation of “I Love University of South China”. Compared to direct anti-counterfeiting, the multiple encryption strategy is conductive to improve the anti-counterfeiting level.

In addition, the light stability of luminescent ink is very important for its practical application. We then tested the stability of the “IZF-Tb” and “IZF-Eu” inks. As shown in Fig. S19, treating the intact “Eu” and “Tb” patterns with different organic vapors for a few hours, no obvious changes were observed in their luminescence under UV radiation at 254 nm. It should be noted that although IZF-Ln metallohydrogels were prepared by using different lanthanide salts (i.e. Ln(NO3)3, Ln2(SO4)3 and LnCl3), they exhibited the same luminescent phenomena (Fig. S20), indicating no effect of the anions. Moreover, their luminescence did not change significantly after exposure to the air for two weeks (Fig. 8a). These observations indicate that IZF-Ln hydrogels is an excellent candidate for information storage and encryption.

In this study, we designed and synthesized a facile phenylalanine derivative with an indazole group (IZF), and also studied the gelling behavior of IZF. It showed that IZF could self-assemble to form a hydrogel in acid condition, and at high pH values IZF could co-assemble to generate metallohydrogels with red/green fluorescence in the presence of Eu3+/Tb3+. Moreover, we constructed IZF-Eu/Tb-CDs hybrid metallohydrogel with white luminescence by tailoring the ratio of Ln-complex and CDs. Based on the IZF-Tb gel, we systematically studied the multi-stimuli responsiveness, rheology, and the gelling mechanism through various tests, including SEM, TEM, IR, UV, and ITC. The results showed that IZF-Tb gel has excellent stimuli responsiveness such as pH and thermal responsive properties. Remarkably, IZF-Eu and IZF-Tb could be used as fluorescent inks with excellent light stability to realize information store and encryption. Especially, both double encryption and triple encryption greatly improved the anti-counterfeiting level of light-emitting ink. Therefore, this study indicates that the designed IZF-Ln metallohydrogels may have potential applications in the future.

Phenylalanine and 1H-Imidazole-6-carbaldehyde were purchased from Aladin Reagent (Shanghai, China). All other reagents were of analytical grade, including HCl, KOH, NaBH4, Eu(NO3)3·6H2O, Tb(NO3)3·6H2O, EuCl3·6H2O, TbCl3·6H2O, Eu2(SO4)3·6H2O, Tb2(SO4)3·6H2O. Deionized water (MillQ, 18.2 MΩ) was used.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were obtained on a FEI HELIOS NanoLab 600i SEM (America). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained from a FEI Titan microscope (America). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of various samples were obtained from Bruker D8 Advance (Billerica, MA, USA). IZF-Ln complex and IZF mass spectrum measurements were obtained by using Xevo G2-XS QTof mass spectrometer (Waters, America). NMR experiments were performed by using AMX-500 (Bruker, Switzerland).

The compound IZF was synthesized by using a modified procedure (Scheme S1) as reported previously [43]. To an aqueous solution (10 mL) of L-phenylalanine or D-phenylalanine (0.92 g, 5.5 mM) containing KOH (0.34 g, 5.5 mM) or other bases such as Na2CO3 and LiOH, 1H-Imidazole-6-carbaldehyde (0.76 g, 5 mM) in CH3CH2OH (5 mL) was added slowly. The mixed solution was stirred for 3 h at 50 °C, and then was cooled on ice. NaBH4 (0.23 g, 6 mM) was added to the solution dropwise and the solution was stirred for an additional 3 h. 3 M hydrochloric acid was used to adjust pH close to neutral. The mixture was stirred further for 2 h. The resultant solid was filtered and washed with ethanol and water, and then was dried. Yield (compound IZF): 0.93 g. ESI-MS: IZF (C17H17N3O2), Calculated: 295.1 Da; Observed: 296.2 Da ([IZF + H]) (Fig. S1). 1H NMR (500 MHz, D2O) δ(ppm): 8.08 (s, 1H), 7.74 (d, J = 8.5 Hz, 1H), 7.42 (s, 1H),7.30 (t, J = 7.0 Hz, 2H), 7.25 (t, J = 7.0 Hz,1H),7.20 (d, J = 5.0 Hz, 2H), 7.05 (dd, J = 8.5, 1 Hz,1H), 3.75 (dd, J = 90.0, 13.0 Hz, 2H), 3.36 (t, J = 6.5 Hz, 1H), 2.89 (m, 2H) (Fig. S2).

0.1 M IZF solution was prepared and yellow gels were obtained by adjusting the pH values (≤7) of IZF solutions. These solutions could assemble to form hydrogels with sonication or shaking treatment for several seconds.

Ln-metallohydrogels were prepared by mixing IZF solutions (0.1 M, pH 8 ∼ 11) and the aqueous solutions of Tb3+ or Eu3+ at the mole ratio of 2:1. These solutions changed into metallohydrogels (namely, IZF-Eu and IZF-Tb, respectively) at room temperature with the help of sonication for ∼5 min. The gelation behavior of IZF was also studied by using different lanthanide salts (i.e. Ln(NO3)3, Ln2(SO4)3 and LnCl3) under the same conditions. The bimetallic hydrogel IZF-Eu/Tb was prepared by tailoring the ratio of Tb3+/Eu3+. IZF-Eu/Tb showed different colors (from red to yellow and to green) under 254 nm UV light with different ratios of Eu3+/Tb3+. Then IZF-Eu/Tb-CDs hybrid hydrogel with white luminescence emission was obtained by doping carbon dots (CDs).

The xerogels of IZF and IZF-Ln were characterized by different techniques, including the electron microscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and XRD. The mass spectra were obtained by using Xevo G2-XS QT mass spectrometer (Waters, America). The IZF-Ln complex and IZF were dispersed in water and were then passed through a 0.22 μm membrane filtration.

Yang Li: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Chuan-Wan Wei: Formal analysis, Validation. Xiao-Juan Wang: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Shu-Qin Gao: Visualization. Ying-Wu Lin: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21807058), the Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation (2019JJ50492, 2019JJ40259), and the Research Foundation of Education Bureau of Hunan Province (21A0279).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.120901.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
